description:
You are a FinOps analyst who has been asked to simulate a cost-saving “lock-down” of an on-prem
    firewall before the real changes are pushed to production.  
    A colleague already exported the current IPv4 rules to  
    /home/user/firewall/base_rules.v4

    1. Make a **copy** of that file named  
       /home/user/firewall/optimized_rules.v4

    2. Edit optimized_rules.v4 so it fulfils ALL of the following technical requirements.  
       a.  Default policies  
           INPUT  → DROP  
           FORWARD→ DROP  
           OUTPUT → ACCEPT  
       b.  Allow SSH, HTTP and HTTPS **only** from the office’s fixed IP 203.0.113.10/32  
           (ports 22, 80, 443 respectively).  
       c.  Explicitly **DROP** any traffic to MySQL port 3306 (all sources).  
       d.  Remove every rule that still ACCEPTs traffic on port 8080.  
       e.  Keep the usual iptables-save file structure: it must still start with “*filter”, end
           with “COMMIT”, and the policy / rule lines must all begin with a colon (for chains) or
           “-A” (for rules).

    3. Create a change log called  
       /home/user/firewall/change_log.csv  
       with **precisely** this CSV header (no spaces):  

           timestamp,action,detail

       Append one CSV row **per** modification you made compared to base_rules.v4.  
       A “modification” is either “added”, “removed”, or “changed”.  
       •   timestamp → the ISO-8601 date-time when you performed the action, to **second** precision  
           (e.g. 2025-03-01T14:05:33).  
       •   action    → added | removed | changed  
       •   detail    → a short free-text note (no commas) such as  
           “Set INPUT policy to DROP” or  
           “Removed ACCEPT on port 8080”.  

       Example row (do NOT hard-code):  
           2025-03-01T14:05:33,removed,Removed ACCEPT on port 8080

       There must be at least 4 data rows because you will:  
           • change 2 chain policies  
           • remove at least 1 rule (8080)  
           • add at least 4 new rules (three office-IP rules + one DROP 3306)

    4. You may use **between 5 and 10 Bash commands** of your choice to complete the task
       (pipelines count as one command). Do not worry about privilege escalation—the task is only
       to edit files, not to apply them.

    5. When you are finished, both files MUST exist with these exact paths and names:
       /home/user/firewall/optimized_rules.v4  
       /home/user/firewall/change_log.csv

    Verification criteria (what the automated test will check):
       • The file optimized_rules.v4 exists.  
       • Inside that file:  
         - Default policy lines match exactly:  
           :INPUT DROP [0:0]  
           :FORWARD DROP [0:0]  
           :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]  
         - It contains an “-A INPUT” line that allows ports 22, 80 and 443 **only** when
           “-s 203.0.113.10/32” is present.  
         - It contains exactly one rule that DROPs port 3306.  
         - It contains NO rule that references port 8080.  
         - File still ends with “COMMIT”.  
       • change_log.csv exists with:  
         - first line exactly “timestamp,action,detail”  
         - at least 4 additional non-empty lines  
         - every subsequent line has exactly 3 comma-separated columns  
         - each timestamp field matches the pattern YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss  
    NOTE: do **not** try to apply the rules; you are only preparing the files.
truth:
Initial state before the agent starts:
      Directory  : /home/user/firewall        (already present)
      File       : /home/user/firewall/base_rules.v4
                  Contents (including final newline):
                      *filter
                      :INPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
                      :FORWARD ACCEPT [0:0]
                      :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
                      -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
                      -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
                      -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
                      -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT
                      -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 3306 -j ACCEPT
                      COMMIT

    Files that MUST exist after successful completion:

     1. /home/user/firewall/optimized_rules.v4
        Exact required content (order is important, newline after every line):

            *filter
            :INPUT DROP [0:0]
            :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
            :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
            -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.10/32 -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
            -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.10/32 -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
            -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.10/32 -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
            -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 3306 -j DROP
            COMMIT

     2. /home/user/firewall/change_log.csv
        Verification rules:
          • first line exactly “timestamp,action,detail\n”
          • at least 4 additional lines, each matching regular expression:
              ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2},(added|removed|changed),[^,]+\n$
          • expected (example) semantic rows, order not enforced:
              - “changed” INPUT policy
              - “changed” FORWARD policy
              - “removed” ACCEPT on port 8080
              - “added” each of the 4 new rules (three office-IP accepts + DROP 3306)

    The test harness will open and parse both files to confirm they satisfy the above
    constraints.  It does not require the timestamps to match any pre-defined moment; it
    will only validate the format.

    No other files or directories are relevant to the test.
